Analytic Philosophy of Mind

Gilbert Ryle

1900 – 1976

The ghost in the machine dismissed: mind as a set of dispositions and abilities.

Preliminary clarification: I have not found an established philosopher of mind by the exact compound name “Gilbert Ryle Jones.” The figure who fits the request is almost certainly Gilbert Ryle: the Oxford philosopher, author of The Concept of Mind (1949), Waynflete Professor of Metaphysical Philosophy, and long-time editor of Mind. I will therefore treat the subject as Ryle’s philosophy of mind and psychology.

1. Central thesis

Gilbert Ryle’s philosophy of mind is best understood as an attempt to dismantle a mistaken picture of mind, not by denying mental life, but by changing the logical grammar through which mental life is described. His target is the idea that a human being consists of two coordinated realities: a public physical body and a private inner mind, where the latter is a hidden arena of thoughts, volitions, images, feelings, and acts of consciousness. Ryle calls this picture the “Ghost in the Machine,” and his main charge is that it is a category mistake: it represents facts about mental life as if they belonged to the same logical type as hidden processes, inner objects, or private episodes.

Ryle’s positive view is not that minds do not exist. Nor is it simply that mental states are bodily movements. Rather, his view is that mental predicates ordinarily describe persons in terms of their abilities, tendencies, skills, styles of conduct, powers of discrimination, capacities for learning, responsiveness to reasons, self-corrective practices, moods, feelings, avowals, and patterns of performance. The mind is not an occult item inside the person; it is the structured intelligibility of the person’s life and conduct. Ryle’s own formulation of his project is “philosophical psychology”: an investigation of the logical behavior of ordinary mental concepts such as learning, trying, wanting, pondering, pretending, seeing, watching, and being perturbed.

The philosophy of mind that can be extracted from Ryle is therefore a person-centered, anti-Cartesian, dispositional, normative, and anti-reductive account of mindedness. It denies both the Cartesian picture of the mind as a private immaterial theatre and the crude behaviorist picture of the mind as mere observable muscular behavior. It also resists later theories that treat mental concepts as standing for hidden computational or neurological truth-makers if those theories make ordinary mental ascription unintelligible.

2. Historical position in philosophy of mind

Ryle occupies a decisive transitional position in twentieth-century philosophy of mind. Before him, the dominant inherited framework was Cartesian dualism: mind and body were treated as fundamentally different kinds of thing, somehow interacting. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy notes that Ryle could still describe Cartesian dualism in 1949 as the “official doctrine,” although anti-dualist sentiment had been growing for decades. After Ryle, contemporary materialism came to predominate, especially with U. T. Place and J. J. C. Smart in the 1950s.

Ryle’s work appears just before the rise of the mind-brain identity theory, functionalism, and cognitive science. The identity theory would argue that mental states are identical with brain states; functionalism would later define mental states by their causal roles among inputs, internal states, and outputs. Functionalism preserves one Rylean insight, namely that mental concepts are deeply connected with behavior and practical role, while rejecting what many took to be behaviorism’s eliminative or reductive tendencies.

But Ryle should not be read simply as a failed precursor to functionalism. His most radical claim is that many standard mind-body problems arise from a prior logical confusion. The question “How does the mind relate to the body?” is already distorted if it assumes that “mind” names an item comparable to “body,” differing only in being private, non-spatial, or immaterial. Ryle’s historical importance lies in shifting attention from metaphysical inventory to the logical geography of mental concepts: what we are actually doing when we call someone intelligent, careful, vain, angry, in pain, imagining, deciding, pretending, or understanding.

This also explains why Ryle is easy to misclassify. He is often placed under “logical behaviorism,” but that label is misleading if it means that mental statements are reducible to statements about bodily movements. Ryle is closer to a philosopher of conceptual practice: he asks how mental concepts work in explanation, assessment, teaching, correction, prediction, biography, law, ethics, and everyday human dealings. The SEP explicitly warns that the common view of Ryle as a philosophical behaviorist is a “fundamental misapprehension” of his work.

3. Ryle’s method: logical geography and category mistakes

Ryle’s method is not primarily empirical psychology, formal logic, or speculative metaphysics. It is conceptual cartography. People are competent users of mental concepts: they can call someone careful, rash, witty, unobservant, self-controlled, insincere, confused, or intelligent. But they may still misunderstand the logical relationships among those concepts. Ryle compares this to someone who knows their way around a parish but cannot draw or read a map of the region. Philosophy supplies the map: it determines the “logical cross-bearings” of concepts.

A category mistake occurs when something is assigned to the wrong logical type. Ryle’s famous example is the visitor who sees colleges, libraries, laboratories, and offices, and then asks where the university is, as though the university were another building alongside the rest. The mistake is not empirical ignorance. It is a misunderstanding of how the term “university” functions. Likewise, Ryle thinks that Cartesian dualism treats “mind” as though it named an additional object or theatre alongside the body, when in fact mental discourse works in a different way.

This is why Ryle insists that exploding a myth is not the same as denying the facts. He is not saying that people do not think, feel, imagine, intend, or suffer pain. He is saying that philosophers have classified these facts under the wrong logical categories. They have treated intelligence, emotion, intention, sensation, and imagination as if they were secret processes or objects in an internal arena. Ryle’s project is to reallocate these facts to the categories in which our actual mental discourse already works.

His method is therefore therapeutic but also systematic. It is therapeutic because it dissolves pseudo-problems created by bad conceptual grammar. It is systematic because it reconstructs the logical types of mental concepts: some are abilities, some are dispositions, some are performances, some are achievements, some are exercises of skill, some are moods, some are sensations, some are avowals, and some are higher-order capacities. There is no single mental essence common to all of them.

4. The “Official Doctrine”: the Cartesian picture of mind

Ryle’s negative target is what he calls the “Official Doctrine.” According to this doctrine, a person has a body in space and a mind outside space. The body is public, mechanical, and observable; the mind is private, non-mechanical, and directly accessible only to itself. Bodily events are motions; mental events are inner episodes. Other people may observe my body, but only I can observe my thoughts, feelings, intentions, and images.

This picture produces a familiar cluster of philosophical problems. First, it produces the mind-body problem: how can non-spatial mental events causally interact with spatial bodily events? Second, it produces the problem of other minds: if mental life is hidden behind behavior, how can anyone know whether another person has thoughts, feelings, or intentions? Third, it produces a special theory of self-knowledge: each subject supposedly has privileged access to their own inner episodes through introspection or consciousness.

Ryle’s response is not to offer a new causal bridge between mind and body. He thinks the demand for such a bridge is already generated by the wrong picture. If mental predicates do not name private inner episodes, then we do not need to explain how those episodes attach themselves to bodily movements. When we say that someone intentionally pulled a trigger, understood a joke, was angry, acted generously, or remembered a tune, we are not ordinarily reporting the occurrence of a hidden inner act plus a visible bodily act. We are characterizing the person’s conduct under a conceptually richer description.

This is why Ryle’s anti-Cartesianism also applies to some later physicalist and computational views. The SEP emphasizes that Ryle’s target was not merely the immateriality of alleged mental processes, but their hiddenness. A theory can repeat the Cartesian mistake even if it replaces immaterial episodes with hidden computations or neural events, provided it treats those hidden items as the real truth-makers of ordinary mental discourse in a way that makes our ordinary use of mental concepts mysterious.

5. Ryle’s positive conception of mind

The positive Rylean conception begins with a shift in the unit of analysis. The primary bearer of mental predicates is not a mind-substance, nor a brain considered in isolation, but the person. We call persons clever, sincere, resentful, careful, calculating, imaginative, or vain. We also describe what persons do: they infer, joke, hesitate, pretend, avoid, remember, wince, explain, revise, promise, and apologize. Mental discourse is embedded in the practices through which we understand, appraise, predict, teach, correct, and coordinate with people.

This does not mean that Ryle denies the brain, the body, or inner episodes. It means that mental concepts are not uniformly names of inner episodes. Many mental predicates are dispositional or capacity-ascribing. To say that someone knows French, is vain, understands chess, is quick-tempered, or is a careful driver is not to say that a particular inner event is now occurring. It is to place the person within a network of abilities, tendencies, liabilities, and appropriate manifestations across indefinitely many possible circumstances. Ryle’s own discussion of dispositions emphasizes that higher-grade human dispositions are not “single-track”: their exercises may take a wide and heterogeneous variety of forms.

The key point is that a mental concept often functions as an inference license. If someone is described as knowing French, we are licensed to expect certain performances: understanding, translating, correcting mistakes, answering questions, following instructions, detecting errors, and so on. But no one performance is necessary or sufficient in isolation. The concept is not reducible to a fixed list of behaviors. Human dispositions are open-ended, context-sensitive, norm-governed, and indefinitely various.

This gives Ryle’s view a distinctive shape. It is not dualist, because it rejects a second private arena. It is not reductive materialist, because it does not identify mental concepts with brain-state concepts. It is not crude behaviorist, because it does not reduce the mental to bodily movement. It is a form of logical dispositionalism about persons, where mindedness consists in the way persons are able, liable, trained, disposed, responsive, and accountable.

6. Intelligence, knowing-how, and the anti-intellectualist legend

Ryle’s treatment of intelligence is one of his most enduring contributions. He attacks what he calls the “intellectualist legend”: the idea that intelligent action is always produced by a prior or accompanying act of theoretical contemplation. On this view, intelligent practice depends on first grasping propositions, rules, or maxims, and then applying them. Ryle argues that this generates a regress. If intelligent action requires prior intelligent contemplation, then the contemplation itself must either be intelligent or unintelligent. If intelligent, it requires a further prior act, and so on.

Ryle’s alternative is that intelligence is often shown directly in performance. A chess player, surgeon, comedian, teacher, pianist, driver, or marksman does not normally first run through a theoretical script and then execute it. Skill is manifested in timing, correction, adaptation, discrimination, sensitivity to context, and ability to deal with variation. Ryle’s example of marksmanship shows the point: whether hitting the target is skill or luck depends not on discovering a hidden inner act, but on the shooter’s broader abilities, record, corrections, explanations, and performance under changed conditions.

The distinction between knowing how and knowing that is therefore central. Knowing how to do something is not merely knowing a set of propositions. One may know every proposition in a manual and still be unable to perform; conversely, one may perform intelligently without being able to state the rules abstractly. The contemporary epistemological debate on knowledge-how traces directly back to Ryle, whose anti-intellectualist view long shaped the received position and continues to influence philosophy, psychology, and neuroscience.

The extracted Rylean doctrine is this: intelligence is not an inner glow added to otherwise mechanical movements. It is a status of performances within a broader pattern of capacity. A performance is intelligent when it is the exercise of a skill, sensitivity, or competence that can be displayed, corrected, taught, varied, criticized, and improved. This account also makes intelligence normative. Intelligent conduct is not merely caused; it is assessable as apt or inept, careful or careless, relevant or irrelevant, successful or bungled.

7. Will, volition, action, and freedom

Ryle’s chapter on the will is a direct attack on the idea that voluntary action is bodily movement caused by a special inner act called a volition. The traditional picture says: I form an idea or desire, then perform an inner volitional act, and that act somehow causes my muscles to move. Ryle rejects this as an extension of the Ghost in the Machine. He explicitly says that rejecting volitions does not abolish the distinction between voluntary and involuntary action; rather, it clarifies that distinction by freeing it from an absurd hypothesis.

For Ryle, an intentional action is not a compound of one physical event and one hidden mental event. It is one episode described under action-relevant concepts. A person frowning intentionally is not undergoing two events, one on the face and another in a private mental theatre. The intentionality lies in the person-level description: the circumstances, purpose, awareness, training, social context, responsibility, and intelligibility of the act.

Ryle’s distinction between causes and reasons or motives is crucial. He argues that explaining an action by citing a motive is not the same as naming an event-cause. Motives are not happenings; they are not the right logical type to be causes. A motive-expression expands into a law-like or dispositional characterization: the person is inclined to act in such-and-such ways under such-and-such conditions. This does not exclude ordinary causes or occasions. Hearing a request may occasion someone to pass the salt; politeness explains why that request led to that response in that person.

Ryle’s account therefore dissolves, rather than solves, a common free-will problem. The problem arises when voluntary action is imagined as a bodily mechanism needing a non-mechanical inner push. Ryle denies the need for such a push. Human action can be causally occasioned and also intelligible under motives, reasons, skills, and responsibilities. Physical causation and person-level explanation do not compete as two forces tugging at the same movement. They answer different questions.

8. Emotion, feeling, mood, and motive

Ryle’s treatment of emotion is pluralistic. He rejects the assumption that emotions are essentially private internal experiences. The word “emotion,” he argues, covers several different kinds of phenomena, including inclinations or motives, moods, agitations, and feelings. These are not all the same logical kind. Inclinations and moods are propensities rather than occurrences; feelings are occurrences, but their role in human conduct is not what standard inner-theatre theories suppose.

This is a powerful move. A Cartesian or introspectionist theory tends to treat anger, fear, jealousy, grief, or hope as private episodes inside consciousness. Ryle instead asks how these concepts function. To say that someone is resentful, generous, timid, ambitious, or proud is often to say something about their standing tendencies, sensitivities, and patterns of response. To say that someone is agitated may concern posture, voice, tempo, attention, impulsiveness, and bodily disturbance. To report a pang, thrill, qualm, or twinge is to report an occurrence, but not necessarily an object privately observed.

Ryle’s account also anticipates a point now common in philosophy of emotion: emotion terms are not uniform labels for one inner natural kind. They can involve affective episodes, expressive behavior, evaluative orientation, practical readiness, social meaning, and bodily feeling. Ryle’s contribution is to insist that philosophical confusion begins when all these are forced into the model of a private mental occurrence.

9. Sensation, perception, and the rejection of inner objects

Ryle’s philosophy of sensation is one of the most controversial parts of his thought. His central claim is that sensations are not privately observed objects. If my shoe pinches, another person cannot feel my tweak; but it does not follow that I observe the tweak as a private object. Ryle’s point is grammatical: I have or feel the sensation, but I do not watch, inspect, or discover it in the way I might observe a robin, a road accident, or a stain on the wall.

He also distinguishes sensation from perception. Perception involves the possibility of getting sensory indications of something; sensation is not itself an act of perceiving, observing, detecting, or knowing. To have a visual sensation is not to stand in a cognitive relation to a special inner visual object. Ryle therefore rejects sense-datum theories that construe perception as awareness of private mental objects.

The extracted Rylean position is subtle. He is not denying pain, twinges, afterimages, itches, or bodily sensations. He is denying that their privacy should be modeled as private observation. The person who feels pain is not a spectator of a private object; the pain is something suffered, had, expressed, reported, endured, attended to, ignored, treated, or complained of. The philosophical error is to turn this into an inner epistemological relation between a subject and an object.

This is also where many critics find Ryle least satisfying. Modern discussions of phenomenal consciousness and qualia often press exactly where Ryle tries to dissolve the issue: what is the subjective character of pain, color experience, or auditory experience? Ryle’s answer is conceptually deflationary; he blocks the move from sensation to inner object. But that may not fully satisfy those who think the problem of phenomenal character survives the rejection of sense-data.

10. Imagination, mental imagery, and the “mind’s eye”

Ryle applies the same strategy to imagination. To visualize a face, hear a tune in one’s head, or picture a childhood home is not literally to see or hear an object in a private theatre. “Seeing in the mind’s eye” does not require the existence of an inner thing seen or an inner act of seeing it. Ryle compares the error to treating a stage murder as a real murder with a victim.

This does not trivialize imagination. It relocates it. Imagining is an ability-laden activity: one can imagine vividly or dimly, faithfully or inaccurately, voluntarily or involuntarily, creatively or repetitively. But the grammar of picturing is not the grammar of seeing. A person can picture a face with eyes shut in the dark; seeing requires appropriate perceptual conditions and public objects. The words may overlap, but the logical roles differ.

Ryle’s view of imagination therefore avoids both eliminativism and inner-picture realism. It allows that people visualize, rehearse tunes, daydream, fantasize, and silently soliloquize. What it denies is that such activities require a private museum of mental images. This is consistent with Ryle’s broader insistence that some episodes may be private in the sense of secret or unreported, without being private in the Cartesian sense of belonging to an inaccessible ontological realm.

11. Self-knowledge, consciousness, and introspection

Ryle’s critique of introspection follows from the same anti-theatrical model. The traditional view says that each person has privileged access to their own mind through an inner perception called consciousness or introspection. Ryle rejects this. He does not deny that we know much about ourselves. He denies that such knowledge is obtained by observing private mental objects or episodes on an internal stage.

His positive account restores “approximate parity” between self-knowledge and knowledge of others. The sorts of things I can find out about myself are largely the same sorts of things I can find out about others: whether I am irritable, confused, hopeful, jealous, inattentive, competent, frightened, or vain. The methods also overlap: memory, evidence, behavior, avowal, expression, correction, comparison, and interpretation. In some respects I know myself better; in others others may know me better.

Ryle’s argument against introspection is partly phenomenological and partly logical. If introspection were an inner observation of mental processes, then theorists should be able to settle disputes about alleged inner acts by simply looking. But controversies about volitions, conscience, or inner operations do not resolve that way. He also invokes a Humean point: some states, such as panic or fury, cannot be coolly scrutinized while they are occurring, because the detached scrutiny would alter or displace the state.

This does not erase first-person authority. Ryle allows that avowals such as “I am in pain,” “I am thinking about the meeting,” or “I feel anxious” have a special role. But their authority is not the authority of an infallible inner eye. It is closer to the role of expression, acknowledgment, unstudied report, and practical self-manifestation. The SEP emphasizes that Ryle does not deny dreams, imaginings, silent soliloquies, musings, or present feelings; he denies that a Cartesian theory of luminous self-observation is needed to account for them.

12. Thinking and the limits of behaviorist readings

Ryle’s later work makes especially clear why he should not be reduced to behaviorism. He recognized that The Concept of Mind had focused heavily on observable criteria because its polemical target was Cartesian hiddenness. But thinking also includes pensive activities that may have little or no overt manifestation: musing, calculating silently, reflecting, hesitating inwardly, composing, or dwelling on a problem. Ryle’s later aim was to avoid both the category mistake of Cartesianism and the category mistake of behaviorism.

His mature position is not “not inner, therefore muscular.” Nor is it “not muscular, therefore inner.” Rather, mental life includes a range of sophisticated activities, omissions, hesitations, exercises, and capacities that cannot be captured by either crude public behavior or occult inner process. He also rejects the view that thinking is simply symbol manipulation; there is no single answer to what thinking consists in, because “thinking” covers many different kinds of activities.

This matters historically. The rise of cognitive science encouraged a new hidden-process theory: intelligent behavior is explained by internal representations and computational operations. Ryle’s critique does not refute cognitive science as empirical research. But it warns against treating hidden computation as the semantic foundation of mental discourse. A model of cognition may explain mechanisms; it does not automatically replace the logical grammar of belief, skill, understanding, judgment, intention, and imagination.

13. Ryle and psychology

Ryle’s relation to psychology is frequently misunderstood. He does not reject empirical psychology. He rejects the idea that psychology is the science of a private non-physical realm. He says that his book has said little about scientific psychology because it is instead an essay in philosophical psychology: it examines ordinary mental concepts used not only by psychologists but also by novelists, historians, teachers, detectives, magistrates, politicians, and ordinary people.

Ryle thinks the historical program of psychology was contaminated by the two-worlds legend. When psychology was imagined as the counterpart to Newtonian physical science, it was cast as the empirical science of non-physical mental phenomena, supposedly discovered by non-sensory inner perception. This picture made psychologists seem like investigators of a private field inaccessible to historians, teachers, friends, biographers, or dramatists.

Against this, Ryle argues that the study of minds is not monopolized by technical psychology. A teacher studying a pupil’s mistakes, a detective studying a suspect’s habits, a novelist depicting jealousy, a strategist studying an opponent’s decisions, and a psychologist measuring reaction time may all be studying minded conduct in different ways. Psychology has specialized tasks, especially where ordinary understanding fails: dreams, slips, illusions, abnormal responses, perceptual distortions, memory failures, and puzzling behavior. But not every explanation of human action is psychological, and not every psychological inquiry is a search for causal explanation.

Ryle’s attitude toward behaviorism is equally nuanced. He accepts the methodological importance of public, repeatable, checkable observation. He also thinks behaviorism helped reveal the shadowiness of many supposed inner-life entities. But he says early behaviorists erred whether they adopted a mechanistic view or merely restricted themselves to public laboratory phenomena while leaving the Cartesian inner world intact.

The result is a distinctive philosophy of psychology. Psychology should not be founded on introspective access to a private realm. Nor should it reduce persons to stimulus-response machines. It should study actual human and animal capacities, performances, failures, learning, perception, memory, emotion, speech, and conduct, while recognizing that many mental concepts belong to wider practices of human understanding and cannot be captured solely in technical psychological vocabulary.

14. Why Ryle is not a crude behaviorist

Behaviorism itself has multiple meanings. In a loose sense, a behaviorist insists that psychological attributions require behavioral criteria. In a stricter doctrinal sense, behaviorism may claim that psychology is the science of behavior rather than the inner mind, and that behavior can be explained without ultimate reference to internal mental events.

Ryle shares the first emphasis but not the crude doctrine. He insists that mental predicates have public criteria of application, but he does not reduce those criteria to simple bodily movements. The SEP notes that Ryle’s problem with behaviorism is that it has too narrow a conception of behavior and observability. Ryle allows dreams, imaginings, silent thinking, and inner episodes; he denies only that they constitute the Cartesian private theatre that generates skepticism about other minds and the semantics of mental language.

A better label for Ryle would be anti-Cartesian logical dispositionalism or normative dispositional philosophy of persons. He is “behaviorist” only in the weak sense that he refuses to sever mental concepts from the criteria by which they are taught, used, challenged, and defended. He is not behaviorist in the strong sense that he treats mindedness as nothing but muscular movement.

15. A reconstructed Rylean philosophy of mind

A comprehensive philosophy of mind extracted from Ryle can be stated as the following interconnected theses.

First, the primary subject of mental predicates is the person. Minds are not ghostly objects inhabiting bodies. We describe persons as intelligent, vain, frightened, hopeful, calculating, imaginative, and sincere. Mental discourse is anchored in the intelligibility of persons and their lives.

Second, mental concepts are logically heterogeneous. There is no single essence called “the mental.” Some mental concepts name abilities, some performances, some achievements, some feelings, some moods, some dispositions, some liabilities, some avowals, some failures, and some exercises of skill.

Third, many central mental predicates are multi-track dispositions. To know, believe, understand, intend, be proud, be irritable, or be careful is not usually to undergo a single inner episode. It is to have a structured propensity whose manifestations may be indefinitely diverse across contexts.

Fourth, intelligence is not the product of prior inner theorizing. Intelligent action is often the direct exercise of skill. Rules and propositions can guide action, but the ability to use rules is itself a practical competence, not something explained by an infinite hierarchy of prior rules.

Fifth, action is not bodily motion plus volition. Voluntary and intentional action must be analyzed through person-level concepts: responsibility, control, purpose, training, attention, opportunity, knowledge, and circumstance. Hidden volitions do not explain action; they duplicate the problem.

Sixth, reasons and motives are not inner event-causes. They are dispositional or law-like explanations of why an action is intelligible as the action of that person. This does not deny ordinary causal occasions; it distinguishes different explanatory roles.

Seventh, self-knowledge is not inner perception. People know themselves through avowal, memory, expression, practical self-understanding, evidence, and interpretation. First-person authority is real but not Cartesian infallibility.

Eighth, other minds are not inferred occult entities. We learn and apply mental concepts through ordinary criteria of conduct, expression, language, correction, and response. The existence of secrecy does not entail the existence of an ontologically private mental world.

Ninth, sensations are not inner objects. Pain, twinges, itches, and sensations are real, but having them is not observing private items. This blocks the move from privacy to an inner theatre.

Tenth, imagination is not perception of inner pictures. Visualizing, rehearsing, daydreaming, and imagining are activities and capacities with their own grammar; they do not require mental objects viewed by an inner eye.

Eleventh, psychology is not the exclusive science of the mind. Empirical psychology is legitimate and valuable, but mental understanding also belongs to teaching, law, biography, history, literature, ethics, politics, and ordinary life.

Twelfth, philosophy of mind should not begin by asking what substance the mind is made of. It should begin by examining the logic of mental concepts and the practices in which they function. Ryle’s philosophy of mind is therefore not a rival empirical psychology but a conceptual discipline that constrains the interpretation of psychology, neuroscience, and cognitive theory.

16. Strengths of Ryle’s position

Ryle’s first major strength is that he exposes the hidden assumptions behind many mind-body problems. Instead of trying to solve the problem of how an immaterial mind moves a material body, he asks why we thought mental agency required an immaterial inner event in the first place. This is a profound shift. It removes the felt need for a metaphysical intermediary between person and action.

His second strength is his analysis of skill and intelligence. The knowing-how/knowing-that distinction remains philosophically fertile because it captures something neglected by intellectualist theories: expertise is not reducible to possession of propositions. Skilled performance involves responsiveness, adaptation, timing, correction, and judgment in action. Contemporary debates over procedural knowledge, embodied cognition, expertise, and artificial intelligence still move in the space Ryle helped define.

His third strength is his resistance to false dichotomies. Ryle refuses the choice between “mind as ghost” and “mind as mechanism.” He also refuses the choice between “nothing but behavior” and “something hidden behind behavior.” This makes his view more flexible than many caricatures suggest. He allows privacy, silent thinking, dreams, and imagery, but denies that they have the metaphysical role assigned to them by Cartesianism.

His fourth strength is methodological. Ryle shows that philosophy of mind must attend to the actual uses of mental vocabulary across ordinary and specialized contexts. A theory that makes it mysterious how teachers, judges, doctors, friends, historians, and children successfully use mental concepts has become detached from its explanandum.

17. Limits and criticisms

The most persistent criticism is that Ryle underestimates phenomenal consciousness. His analysis of pain and sensation blocks the sense-datum model, but many philosophers think there remains a real question about what pain feels like, what visual experience is like, or why conscious experience exists at all. Ryle can reply that these questions are often distorted by inner-object grammar, but the reply may not fully satisfy contemporary qualia theorists.

A second criticism is that Ryle may underplay the explanatory importance of hidden mechanisms. Cognitive science, neuroscience, and psychiatry often explain conduct by appealing to processes not directly visible in ordinary behavior. Ryle need not deny such processes, but his polemic against hiddenness can appear too sweeping unless carefully distinguished from a rejection of empirically warranted subpersonal mechanisms.

A third criticism concerns dispositions. Ryle’s multi-track dispositional analysis is rich, but it can be difficult to specify precisely. What counterfactuals define a belief, skill, mood, or character trait? How do we handle masking conditions, unusual contexts, self-deception, repression, weakness of will, or systematic social performance? Ryle’s answer is deliberately anti-formalist, but that can frustrate those seeking a strict theory.

A fourth criticism is that Ryle’s philosophy is stronger as a critique than as a constructive ontology. He tells us not to reify the mind, not to posit volitions, not to treat images as inner pictures, not to construe sensations as private objects. But what, positively, are persons, capacities, norms, and dispositions in the natural world? Later philosophers often wanted a more explicit metaphysical account.

These criticisms are serious, but they do not make Ryle obsolete. They show that Ryle is not offering a complete neuroscience, psychology, or metaphysics of consciousness. His contribution is different: he clarifies the conceptual terrain on which those disciplines operate.

18. Final assessment

Ryle’s philosophy of mind can be summarized in one sentence: the mind is not a hidden thing inside the body, but the organized field of a person’s capacities, dispositions, performances, sensitivities, avowals, feelings, and norm-governed ways of living.

Historically, Ryle stands at the point where analytic philosophy of mind decisively turns against Cartesian dualism but before it settles into identity theory, functionalism, computationalism, or contemporary consciousness studies. His greatness lies in showing that the inherited mind-body problem may not be a deep metaphysical mystery but a conceptual confusion generated by misclassifying mental concepts. His enduring importance lies in the fact that many later theories, even physicalist ones, can still repeat the Cartesian structure if they treat mental life as essentially hidden behind ordinary human practice.

A rigorous Rylean philosophy of mind is therefore neither dualism nor eliminativism, neither introspectionism nor crude behaviorism. It is a philosophy of minded persons: practical, dispositional, expressive, norm-laden, socially intelligible, and conceptually plural. Its governing question is not “Where is the mind?” but “What are we doing when we describe, explain, teach, criticize, predict, and understand people as minded beings?”

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
